topic: Someone discovers their friend has been lying about their job
raw_output:
[story 1]

The lukewarm coffee tasted like ash in Maya’s mouth. She stared at the spreadsheet on her laptop, the numbers blurring into meaningless streaks of grey. Liam’s story had unravelled like cheap thread. He’d been telling her for months that he was a senior architect at a prestigious firm, a position that explained his sudden influx of expensive dinners and his air of effortless confidence. Now, the inconsistencies—a missed project deadline he claimed to have managed, a phone call he swore was with a client but whose timestamp didn't match the location he’d described—were piling up. Maya rubbed her temples, the pressure behind her eyes making the room swim slightly. When Liam finally admitted he was just freelancing, taking on small, vague contracts, the energy drained out of her entirely. She didn't shout. She just closed the laptop with a soft, deliberate click, the sound echoing too loudly in the quiet apartment.

[story 2]

"So, this whole 'corporate ladder' thing is just... a story?" Sarah managed, her voice coming out thin and reedy. She leaned back in the worn armchair, the springs protesting beneath her weight. Every muscle in her neck felt tight, coiled and unresponsive. She had spent the last hour piecing together the gaps in Alex’s narrative—the phantom company logos, the excuses that sounded rehearsed. When Alex finally confessed he was working a completely different, much less impressive gig, the sudden deflation was physical. Sarah felt a heavy lethargy settle over her limbs, making it difficult to lift her gaze from the dusty windowpane. She picked at a loose thread on the sofa cushion, the action slow and unmotivated, as if moving required an impossible expenditure of will.

[story 3]

I watched Ben pace the small living room, his movements jerky and agitated. The silence between his frustrated sighs felt vast and suffocating. He’d been so insistent about his career trajectory, painting a picture of upward mobility that felt increasingly hollow. I had seen the discrepancies myself—the way he avoided specific questions, the nervous flick of his eyes whenever the topic turned to his actual employment. When the truth finally surfaced, stripped bare of all the flattering embellishments, Ben just stopped pacing. He sank onto the edge of the ottoman, his shoulders slumping forward as if the weight of the revelation had physically crushed him. He didn't look up, just stared at the floor, a profound stillness settling over him that mirrored the heavy atmosphere in the room.
